concise:
Ayah 1–2 · linked video: 03. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 1-2b)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 1-2

Ayah 1

ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ

The Surah begins with a single, powerful word: الرَّحْمَـٰنُ . This opening establishes something profound about how we should approach the Quran itself. Allah ﷻ begins by identifying Himself through one of His most comprehensive qualities—Rahman—rather than simply using His name “Allah.”

When we say “Allah,” all of His attributes manifest together: His power, His authority, His ability to punish, His ability to avenge. But when He says “Rahman,” one quality is highlighted above all others: care. This isn’t just any kind of care—it’s comprehensive, all-encompassing care that anticipates needs before they arise.

Understanding Rahman Beyond “Mercy”

The English word “mercy” doesn’t fully capture what Rahman means. In English literature, mercy typically means the absence of punishment. When soldiers show mercy, it means they didn’t kill you. When someone begs for mercy, they’re asking not to be hurt. Mercy, as understood in English, often means something bad didn’t happen rather than something good did happen.

But Rahman comes from the Arabic root رحم , which means “womb.” The womb of a mother has nothing to do with sparing someone from harm. Rather, it represents being cared for from every angle. The baby in the womb doesn’t even know what difficulties exist—the mother handles them. The baby’s entire world is being taken care of by the mother, and even when the baby causes the mother pain, she bears it willingly.

This is the closest thing in human imagination to what Allah ﷻ describes as one of His qualities. While there is no true comparison to Allah ﷻ, even a hadith of the Prophet ﷺ makes a connection between the womb and mercy. Allah ﷻ says to the womb: “I named you by My name.”

Rahman means He takes care of every angle. All of your concerns are handled. This is the capacity in which Allah ﷻ chose to introduce the teaching of the Quran.

What This Tells Us About the Quran

This opening sets the stage for how we’re supposed to think about the Quran. Ask the average Muslim today what their impression of the Quran is. What do they tell you? Often, you’ll hear about what’s forbidden, about punishment, about hellfire. The last thing you hear people associate with the Quran—from Muslims and non-Muslims alike—is that it’s an act of love and care.

Non-Muslims may call it barbaric or merciless. Even Muslims sometimes struggle with passages about punishment, questioning how a book that calls its Author so merciful can speak about burning in hellfire.

But this opening declares something essential: the teaching of the Quran by Ar-Rahman is, in fact, the ultimate mercy.

Ayah 2

عَلَّمَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ

The Mercy in Teaching Over Time

Allah ﷻ says: عَلَّمَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ (He taught the Quran). The choice of the word علّم is significant. This verb form indicates teaching that happens over time, little by little. Allah ﷻ could have used other verbs—He could have said He “gave” the Quran or “sent down” the Quran. But He says He “taught” it, and this teaching happened gradually, with patience.

This tells us something about how we should approach learning the Quran ourselves. If Allah ﷻ took time to teach it, we should be comfortable taking time to learn it. There’s no rush to finish it, no need to just be done with it.

A Three-Layer Approach to Studying the Quran

For the average Muslim who decides to become a student of the Quran, a three-layer approach can be beneficial:

First layer: A brief overview of the Quran from beginning to end, getting a general understanding of its message.

Second layer: A more detailed study of tafsir, going slower, taking more time with each ayah. If it takes years to complete this journey, that’s perfectly fine.

Third layer: An elaborate, deep study where you really examine the depths of meaning. It’s acceptable if it takes months to study a single story. This patience is better than rushing through and overlooking countless treasures.

Learning isn’t the only relationship we have with the Quran, but if we’re going to learn it, we should be comfortable with these layers of depth.

The Meaning of “Al-Quran”

Even the name القرآن contains lessons for us. The word comes from the root قرأ, which means “to read.” But Quran is an intensified form—it means reading itself, or to read extensively and repeatedly.

This has two implications. First, Allah ﷻ taught reading itself. The fact that you and I can read anything is from His teaching. But Quran also clearly refers to this specific Book.

Some linguists combine both meanings and suggest something beautiful: nobody has truly experienced reading itself until they’ve read the Quran. The ultimate reading, the climax of what reading should be, is the Quran. This is why it’s called Al-Quran—it represents reading in its highest form.

The Need for Repeated Reading

The structure of the word القرآن already tells us we cannot learn this Book with just one reading. You’ll need to read and reread and reread again. You can’t say, “I read the whole Quran, I know what it says.” A Muslim might read it for twenty years and only then begin to say, “I’m starting to understand what it says.”

Consider the example of Professor Abdel Haleem, who earned his PhD in Balagha and Tafsir from Al-Azhar in 1953 and has been studying the Quran full-time since then. After decades of scholarship, he said, “Last year, I finally began to feel like I’m starting to understand this book.”

There’s no end to the depth this Book can take you to. It’s an endless ocean. Allah ﷻ taught this endless ocean—this is the privilege He has given to all of us.

The Ultimate Education

When someone accepts that this is what they will learn, they’ve accepted Allah ﷻ as their Teacher. They’ve embarked on a journey that is the ultimate education. Any real education consists of reading, and the ultimate reading—in Allah’s ﷻ own definition—is the Quran itself.

This is what it means that Ar-Rahman taught Al-Quran: the Most Caring, the One who provides for every need from every angle, has given us the ultimate Book and taken the time to teach it gradually, with patience and care.

Ayah 3–4 · linked video: 04. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 3-4)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 3

Ayah 3

خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ

After declaring that Ar-Rahman taught the Quran, Allah ﷻ states: “He created the human being.” From a grammatical perspective, this continues the pattern established in the surah. Ar-Rahman remains the subject, and “created the human being” serves as the second predicate. This structure emphasizes that even the act of creation itself is an expression of divine mercy.

Many people struggle with faith by questioning: Why would God create us? How is existence itself an act of mercy? Yet revelation declares precisely this - that our very lives are gifts of divine compassion. What stands out remarkably in this passage is the broken chronology. Allah ﷻ created humanity first, and teaching came later in time. Yet He mentions teaching the Quran before mentioning the creation of human beings. This deliberate sequencing reveals priority rather than timeline. Allah ﷻ establishes that teaching the Quran represents an even greater act of His love and mercy than giving us life itself. The revelation is a bigger gift than the very breath we take.

Muhammad ash-Sha’rawi offered profound insight into this sequencing. He explained that the human being resembles a sophisticated device that Allah ﷻ manufactured. Every manufacturer creates products with purpose and provides an instruction manual. Before any product is manufactured in the world, blueprints come first - specifications of what the product looks like, what it does, and its best use. Even a simple household iron comes with instructions warning against certain misuses.

Similarly, every product necessitates best practices for optimal usage. The manual for humanity is revelation. It precedes in priority because without the manual, we cannot know our purpose. Imagine possessing a remarkable device but having no understanding of what it does or how to use it. This describes life without revelation - a precious gift sitting idle because its owner lacks knowledge of its function. Life becomes purposeless without divine guidance showing us what to use it for.

The Meaning of Insan

The Arabic word الإنسان carries profound linguistic significance. Scholars identify two possible etymological origins, each revealing essential truths about human nature.

The first origin relates to uns, meaning compassion and affection. This contrasts with wahsh, which describes wild animals and their lack of compassion. A predatory animal tears through its prey viciously because it operates purely on instinct, without mercy. The human being, however, possesses the unique capability to show mercy - to children, to fellow humans, and remarkably, even to other species. Humans can care for animals, provide for them, and treat them with gentleness. This capacity for compassion defines our humanity.

The word insan captures both sides of this reality: humans are capable of giving love and desperately need receiving love. Other creatures lack our emotional complexity. A cat survives whether it forms relationships or not. A lion experiences no psychological distress over social bonds. Birds require no therapy. But human beings carry emotional needs - we struggle when feeling unappreciated or disconnected. No other creature shares this dual nature of both offering and requiring affection.

This creates perfect symmetry with Allah’s attribute of being Ar-Rahman, the Ultimately Loving. Our ultimate need finds its fulfillment in His ultimate provision. Revelation offers us His love while simultaneously teaching us how to properly deliver that love throughout humanity. It addresses the deepest need written into human nature.

The second argued origin connects insan to the word nisyan, meaning forgetfulness. The human being is inherently forgetful. Logic dictates that whoever forgets frequently requires frequent reminders. The Quran identifies itself as a reminder more than any other description. Throughout its verses, it continuously calls itself “dhikr” - reminder, reminder, reminder.

Because human beings are forgetful creatures, we desperately needed a powerful reminder. Allah ﷻ sent down the Quran as precisely what humanity requires. This represents tremendous care and mercy - that He would provide exactly what His creation needs. When someone complains that the Quran repeats itself, the response is straightforward: Yes, because repetition is necessary for those who forget, and humans are by definition forgetful creatures.

This forgetfulness extends beyond trivial matters like names and addresses. We forget obligations, responsibilities, and purpose. A student with an exam in three days sits playing video games until a roommate reminds him of the approaching test. He knew about the exam but forgot because human beings conveniently forget what we prefer not to remember. When reality feels harsh, forgetting becomes easier than confronting it.

We avoid thinking about difficult truths until forced to face them. Nobody wants to dwell on harsh realities. Contemplating Judgment Day - standing before Allah ﷻ with all deeds exposed - feels overwhelming, so we find it convenient not to think about it. We need reminders because this forgetfulness is built into our nature. This is the dhikr of Allah ﷻ, this is the Quran.

Ayah 4

عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ

Allah ﷻ adds a unique quality specific to human beings: “He taught him al-bayan.” This marks the second mention of teaching in the surah. First came “He taught the Quran,” and now “He taught him al-bayan.” A critical distinction appears in the Arabic grammar. The first teaching had no specified recipient - Allah ﷻ simply stated He taught the Quran without saying to whom. But when teaching al-bayan, He specifies with the pronoun “hu” (him): “allamahu al-bayan” - He taught him al-bayan.

The Quran is not restricted to human beings. Later in this very surah, we learn of two audiences: humans and jinn. The teaching of the Quran is universal. But al-bayan was given as a gift specifically to human beings.

Understanding Bayan

We know birds speak - the Quran shows us this. We know ants speak. Angels speak with Allah ﷻ. Jinn speak about other jinn speaking. All these creatures possess kalam (speech). But they do not possess bayan. Bayan was uniquely given to the human being.

Both kalam and bayan translate as “speech” in English, but a crucial distinction separates them. Bayan derives from the root “ba-ya-na,” which fundamentally means to separate. From this root comes “bayna” (between) - the space between two things. “Bana yabnu” means to build by separating and organizing components. Bayan means the ability to separate ideas, to separate syllables, to separate paragraphs, chapters, subjects, and books. It represents the capacity to organize and articulate learning through separation.

Human learning operates through cycles of separation and combination. When faced with a complex problem, we break it into its most basic parts. Someone looking at Arabic script for the first time sees only confusing characters. Learning begins by separating: first individual letters, then letters joining into words, words forming phrases, phrases creating sentences, sentences building paragraphs. Human beings were given the ability to articulate in more sophisticated ways than any other creature. This is bayan - sophisticated, clear, organized communication.

As a result of this gift, humanity produces literature. We write about abstract concepts like philosophy. We compose theses on physics, chemistry, microbiology, engineering, and computer programming. We articulate code that isn’t even human language. All intellectual output throughout human history - every university, every library, every written work - represents the manifestation of this single gift Allah ﷻ calls al-bayan.

In Arabic, verbs indicating clarity relate to separation, while words describing confusion relate to fusion and merging. A confused matter is described as “multabis” - fused together, merged. When someone speaks by jumbling syllables, clarity disappears because syllables were not properly separated from each other. Tajweed - the science of reciting Quran clearly - emphasizes what above all else? Ensuring every letter remains separate from every other letter. Don’t consume one letter’s sound by pushing it into another. Give every syllable its full right. This is bayan.

The Argument Against the Mushrikeen

This particular mention of bayan carries powerful argumentative force. The final objection of the Quraysh mushrikeen, after exhausting other rationales to disregard Islam, reduced to: “I’ll believe it when I see it.” They demanded visual proof.

Yet this gift of bayan demonstrates that human beings possess the capability of being convinced through speech - a capacity superior to being convinced through sight alone. An animal sees smoke and runs from it, or smells it, or feels heat and retreats. But a human being hears a radio announcement: “There’s a fire, please don’t go in that direction.” You’re driving on the highway, making good time, when traffic radio warns that five miles ahead there’s a major accident that will cause at least four hours of delay. Will you take the exit, or will you say, “I don’t believe in this radio report. I need to see the traffic jam myself”?

You won’t demand that a hadith scholar verify the traffic jam’s authenticity. You won’t reject the information because it comes from non-Muslims. You’ll trust the reliable source and take the exit. You didn’t see the traffic, yet you were convinced through speech from what you consider trustworthy. That source did you a favor by informing you of trouble ahead, whether visible or not.

Human beings can be convinced through speech. Can animals be convinced through speech? No matter how many dog shows portray animals understanding complex communication, a dog cannot process: “There’s trouble three miles down the road - take a left past the gas station.” If a fire alarm announces throughout this building, “Ladies and gentlemen, this is not a drill. There’s a fire. Please calmly leave the building,” every person capable of understanding will respond. But a cat in the building, a grasshopper, any other creature will not move. They cannot benefit from bayan. Their capacity to appreciate communication remains severely limited.

Because human beings are more sophisticated and evolved in communication, we are capable of benefiting from higher means of conviction. The final revelation determined that human beings should meet their potential by being convinced through bayan rather than demanding only visual proof.

When someone declares, “The only way I will believe is when I see,” they essentially claim to be no different from an animal in their path to conviction. An animal believes when it sees, smells, or tastes - otherwise it does not believe. You are a human being. You were given the gift of bayan. Denouncing the Quran as “just speech” while demanding visual proof means disregarding your own gift. It means rejecting the very capacity that defines your humanity. This forms a powerful argument in the opening of this surah.

Bayan as Clarity

Bayan also means clarity itself - the ability to see clarity, to clarify things for others, and to appreciate clarity when encountered. This represents a gift human beings possess: not merely the ability to speak, but the capability to pursue clarity and deliver it. The Quran is designed to become clear to those who genuinely engage with it. When you study it authentically and certain prerequisites are met, you should be able to perceive its clarity independently. “Allamahu al-bayan” - He taught the human being clear, articulate speech.

Respecting Speech as We Respect Quran

A final crucial reflection emerges from these ayahs. Allah ﷻ declares He taught the Quran. Across the Muslim world, Muslims demonstrate one consistent behavior toward the Quran: respect. We cannot tolerate its disrespect. This manifests in Quran recitation competitions, in decorating displayed verses in our homes, in handling the mushaf only in a state of purity, in wrapping it carefully and placing it on high shelves. These are all acts of respect toward the Quran.

We respect it because Allah ﷻ taught it. But in this passage, Allah ﷻ says He taught two things: He taught the Quran, and He taught al-bayan. If we respect the Quran because He taught it, what else should command our high regard? Speech itself. The ability to communicate, to articulate, to express clarity - this deserves respect just as the Quran deserves respect.

This principle appears throughout the Quran. When Allah ﷻ taught Adam (AS) the names, each noun represented continuation of what Allah ﷻ taught. Faith itself should transform how we view language. Once you possess iman, filthy language becomes intolerable. Corrupt speech becomes terrible. You cannot engage in it. Why? Because faith brings respect for revelation, which automatically brings respect for speech.

This means we must respect what emerges from our mouths. The obligation extends beyond avoiding lying and backbiting. It encompasses obscenity, foul language, and useless speech. The worthless comments Muslims leave under YouTube videos, the pointless Facebook arguments, the endless meaningless texting - all represent disrespect for the gift of bayan. Someone might argue technically: “This isn’t speech because I didn’t say it, I just typed it.” That’s sophistry. Speech is speech, whether spoken or written.

Have respect for it. Allah ﷻ taught you bayan. Honor that gift.

Ayah 4–9 · linked video: 05. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 4-9)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 4-9

Ayah 4

عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ

He taught him articulate speech.

The teaching of بَيَان - articulate, clear expression - represents one of humanity’s most profound gifts from Allah ﷻ. This divine instruction encompasses far more than the mere ability to produce sounds or form words. It is the capacity for sophisticated communication, the power to express complex thoughts, emotions and ideas with clarity and precision.

In our modern era, we witness a paradox: human beings are communicating more than ever before through countless digital platforms, yet the quality and sophistication of this communication continues to decline. Ancient civilizations treasured poetry and storytelling as their primary forms of entertainment - works so refined they are now studied as classical literature in universities. Today’s entertainment, by contrast, often insults human intellect with its lack of depth and sophistication.

Muslims must recognize that this gift of speech carries immense responsibility. The poet wisely observed that every writer will experience death, but what they have written remains throughout time. In our digital age, this truth has intensified exponentially. What we type and submit to servers can be read by thousands, even years after we die - earning us either continuous rewards or ongoing punishment. This makes guarding our speech more critical than ever.

The manifestations of البَيَان include several key responsibilities. First, we must avoid spreading useless content. Much of what fills our screens - particularly in comments sections - serves no beneficial purpose. Second, we must refrain from mockery that crosses boundaries, particularly when it becomes condescending toward other peoples, ethnicities or races. While some humor remains acceptable, comedy that relies primarily on obscenity or demeaning others falls outside the bounds Allah ﷻ has set for us.

Muslims have the creativity and intellect to develop alternative forms of halal entertainment that don’t cross these lines. Sophisticated humor exists that doesn’t require vulgarity or mockery - it simply requires more thought and refinement.

This ayah also establishes a foundation for linguistic appreciation. Allah ﷻ takes credit for teaching all speech, all languages. This means that Arabic, Punjabi, Urdu, English and every human language ultimately comes from Him. While He honored Arabic by choosing it as the language of revelation - giving it a supreme status - this doesn’t mean other languages are inferior or worthy of disrespect. Just as Allah ﷻ gave revelation to the Arabs without declaring other nations inferior, He honored Arabic without dishonoring other tongues.

This understanding should inspire Muslims toward multilingualism. Learning multiple languages expands our thinking, exposes us to different forms of literature and makes us broader thinkers. Throughout Islamic history, wherever Islam traveled, it introduced Arabic but never eliminated the local languages. Persian remained in Iran, Urdu developed in the Indian subcontinent, and local languages flourished alongside Arabic scholarship. This is the beautiful manifestation of respecting all speech as part of divine creation.

Ayah 5

ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ

The sun and the moon run by precise calculation.

After establishing humanity’s unique gift of articulate speech, Allah ﷻ directs our attention to celestial bodies that demonstrate another divine quality: perfect discipline. The word حُسْبَان comes from حساب , meaning calculation, but appears here in an intensified form indicating extreme precision. The sun and moon don’t merely follow calculations - they are constrained by meticulous, unfailing schedules.

This ayah presents a powerful comparison. Human beings represent the pinnacle of Allah’s ﷻ creation, yet even “lesser” creations like the sun and moon possess a quality we must learn from: absolute respect for time and unwavering discipline. These celestial bodies never deviate from their appointed courses, never arrive late, never fail in their responsibilities.

Consider how our entire Islamic practice depends on this celestial discipline. Our daily prayers - the structure that brings order to our days - function based on the sun’s position. Our lunar calendar, which determines Ramadan and Hajj, depends on the moon’s precise cycles. Between these two heavenly bodies lies our complete schedule of worship: the daily rhythm and the annual calendar.

This reveals a profound teaching embedded throughout Makkan Quran. Allah ﷻ wants us to observe the good qualities present in all of His creation, then internalize those qualities in our own lives. When we study the sun and moon’s discipline, we’re not meant to simply admire it from a distance. Rather, we should recognize that the One who manufactured us deliberately designs His creations with discipline. If that’s His design signature, and He created us, then He expects the same discipline from us.

The word حُسْبَان carries a second, more ominous meaning: it can also denote great destruction that arrives at a precise time. This dual meaning adds crucial texture to the surah’s message. Yes, the sun and moon follow their calculated paths, but they’re also approaching a pre-calculated destruction. Imagine a giant clock tower in perfect working order, but with one bolt gradually loosening. When that bolt finally gives way, everything collapses. The sun and moon remain in their courses for now, but they stand at the very edge of their ordained destruction.

Why mention this here? Because this surah opened with the greatest sign of Judgment Day - the revelation of the Quran. The splitting of the moon, referenced in Surah Al-Qamar, represents another major sign. Now, having begun with these signs, the only major event remaining is the destruction of the sun and moon themselves - the arrival of Judgment Day. For those who demand to see before believing, Allah ﷻ points out that the final, ultimate sign is imminent.

Ayah 6

وَٱلنَّجْمُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدَانِ

And the stars and the trees prostrate.

This ayah pairs two elements that dominated the visual landscape of the Arabs: النَّجْم (the stars) and الشَّجَر (the trees). In the desert environment, these represented the primary sources of beauty and wonder. At night, Arabs gazed upon decorated skies filled with stars - their guide for navigation, their inspiration for poetry, their companion in the vast darkness. By day, in that same harsh desert, spotting a tree meant finding precious shade and life - a sight of profound beauty and relief.

Multiple interpretations explain how stars and trees prostrate. Some scholars note that shooting stars appear to bow downward in sajdah, and fruit-bearing trees bend their branches low, as if in prostration. Others understand this prostration as the cosmic submission all creation maintains before its Creator - everything in the universe operates according to divine command.

However, there’s another powerful dimension to this imagery connected to يوم القيامة (the Day of Resurrection). These two magnificent sights that the Arabs couldn’t take their eyes off - the stars above and the trees around them - will fall on Judgment Day. The stars will plummet from the sky, and the trees will topple over, both collapsing in ultimate submission before Allah ﷻ. The very things humanity looks up to and admires will demonstrate their complete dependence on their Creator.

Ayah 7

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ رَفَعَهَا وَوَضَعَ ٱلْمِيزَانَ

And the sky - He raised it and established the balance.

Moving beyond the stars themselves, Allah ﷻ now draws attention to السَّمَاء (the sky) that contains them. He emphasizes that He رَفَعَهَا (raised it) - elevated it to its magnificent height. This raising represents perfect construction, flawless engineering on a cosmic scale.

Then comes the crucial statement: وَوَضَعَ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (and He established the balance). Allah ﷻ placed الميزان (the scale, the balance) in that elevated sky. This balance refers to the precise equilibrium maintaining the universe - the sun in its place, the moon in its orbit, the stars in their positions, all celestial bodies operating in perfect harmony.

This ayah initiates a series of three consecutive verses about الميزان (balance/scales), each building on the previous one. First, Allah ﷻ establishes that He created balance in the heavens. This cosmic balance becomes the foundation for understanding what comes next.

Ayah 8

أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْمِيزَانِ

So that you do not transgress in the balance.

Here Allah ﷻ makes the direct connection between cosmic balance and human behavior. He created the heavenly scales in perfect equilibrium أَلَّا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْمِيزَانِ (so that you do not transgress, violate or cause corruption in the balance).

This represents the Quran’s profound methodology: observe creation’s qualities, then embody them in your life. Just as the sun and moon taught us discipline, the balanced sky now teaches us justice and fairness.

Consider the grocery store owner who adjusts his scales to shortchange customers, earning extra profit through deception. Or the corrupt official who stops buses of pilgrims heading to Hajj, demanding bribes from each passenger - turning the sacred season into personal profit. Before committing such injustices, the Quran’s argument is elegant and powerful: look up at the sky.

Is the sky still balanced? Is the sun still in its place? Is the moon maintaining its orbit? Then the balance of the universe remains intact. This should remind the potential wrongdoer that Allah ﷻ loves balance. He designed everything with fairness and precision. He doesn’t approve of cheating, of things being pushed ahead of their time, of scales being manipulated.

The intact cosmic balance serves as both reminder and warning: while this balance holds, we still have the opportunity to establish balance in our own dealings. When this universal balance finally falls apart on Judgment Day, our personal scales will be raised - the scales weighing our deeds. One of these two scales must exist: either the cosmic balance remains and we have a chance to act justly, or that balance collapses and our deeds are weighed.

Ayah 9

وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلْوَزْنَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا۟ ٱلْمِيزَانَ

And establish weight with justice, and do not fall short in the balance.

The command becomes explicit and direct. أَقِيمُوا۟ (Establish, uphold, maintain) - the same powerful verb used for establishing prayer - now applies to ٱلْوَزْنَ (the weight, the scale). We must uphold scales بِٱلْقِسْطِ (with justice, with fairness).

Justice stands as a core principle throughout Makkan Quran, and Allah ﷻ keeps it general because every human being inherently understands what justice means. It’s written into our فطرة (natural disposition). Then comes the prohibition: وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا۟ ٱلْمِيزَانَ (and do not cause loss in the balance, don’t shortchange the scales).

This third mention of الميزان completes the sequence: the cosmic balance exists, we must not violate balance, and we must actively establish just scales while avoiding any shortchanging.

These principles apply across every dimension of life. Consider the student whose family or community has invested in their Islamic education, paying tuition and trusting them to dedicate themselves fully to learning. An unspoken but real agreement exists - a matter of fairness. The supporters provide financial backing, and the student provides sincere effort and dedication.

When that student sleeps late, skips assignments, watches movies instead of reviewing material after a poor exam performance, they’re not merely disappointing a teacher or falling short of a curriculum. They’re breaking الميزان - violating a trust, being unfair in an agreement that demanded their best effort. This becomes a matter of conscience that must be addressed.

The beautiful reminder? You don’t need a teacher’s lecture to remember this responsibility. What should you do instead? Look at the sky. The balanced heavens remind us: fairness still matters, justice still stands, and Allah ﷻ who maintains that cosmic balance expects us to maintain balance in our trusts, our dealings, our commitments and our character.

Ayah 10–14 · linked video: 06. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 10-14)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 10-14

Ayah 10

وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَضَعَهَا لِلْأَنَامِ

And the earth - He laid it down for all creatures.

Allah ﷻ continues His enumeration of favors by drawing our attention to the earth itself. The word الأنام encompasses all manner of creatures - human beings, animals, insects, fish and everything in between. This universal provision demonstrates that Allah’s mercy extends to all living beings, not just humans.

What’s remarkable here is that Allah ﷻ didn’t just create the earth as a bare platform for survival. He prepared it specifically for life, laying it down with intention and care. The earth was designed and fashioned with purpose - to sustain countless forms of life simultaneously.

Ayah 11

فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَٱلنَّخْلُ ذَاتُ ٱلْأَكْمَامِ

In it are fruits and date palms with covered bunches.

Allah ﷻ moves from the general provision of the earth to specific blessings found within it. The word فَاكِهَة for fruit is linguistically connected to فَكِهَ , which means to be happy or joyful. This connection reveals something profound about Allah’s provision - He didn’t merely give us food for survival. He gave us food that brings us joy.

Consider this carefully: nutrition alone would be sufficient for survival. People in hospitals on strict medical diets receive all necessary nutrients, but the food is often tasteless and unenjoyable. Yet Allah ﷻ, out of His pure mercy, created fruits that not only nourish our bodies but delight our palates. Good taste isn’t necessary for survival - it’s an extra gift, a sign of divine generosity.

The specific mention of date palms (النَّخْل) holds cultural and universal significance. For Arabs, palm trees symbolized beauty and prosperity. Even today, the most luxurious vacation destinations worldwide feature palm trees prominently. California’s expensive real estate, tropical resorts, exotic getaways - they all showcase palm trees as symbols of beauty and comfort. The advertising industry spends millions researching how to sell products, and when they want to convey paradise or luxury, what do they show? Palm trees.

The phrase ذَاتُ ٱلْأَكْمَامِ - “with covered bunches” - refers to how dates grow wrapped in natural sheaths. This detail points to something we often overlook: Allah ﷻ didn’t just give us food, He gift-wrapped it. Think about the orange peel, the banana peel, the watermelon rind. These natural coverings protect the fruit, but they also present it beautifully. The brilliant colors and pleasant scents of fruit peels are so appealing that we use them as inspiration for gift wrapping and presentation.

There’s a modern dimension to appreciate here as well. The food packaging industry is worth trillions of dollars globally, but it contributes significantly to environmental pollution through plastic bottles, aluminum cans and synthetic wrappers. Allah’s packaging, however, is perfect. When you throw an orange peel or banana skin into your garden, does it poison the earth or nourish it? It feeds the soil and facilitates further life. Even the peel has benefits - it’s biodegradable, nutritious for the earth and part of a sustainable cycle.

Ayah 12

وَٱلْحَبُّ ذُو ٱلْعَصْفِ وَٱلرَّيْحَانُ

And grain with husks and aromatic plants.

Allah ﷻ continues cataloging His gifts by mentioning الْحَبّ - grain or seeds that grow in fields. The phrase ذُو ٱلْعَصْف refers to grain that has husks or an outer covering, similar to how wheat or rice grows.

But notice what comes next: وَٱلرَّيْحَان . The word الرَّيْحَان comes from ريح, meaning wind or breeze, and refers to fragrant plants and sweet-smelling winds. If you’ve ever visited farmland during harvest season, you know there’s a beautiful scent in the air. (Avoid planting season when fertilizers are being applied, but harvest time brings a wonderful fragrance that beautifies entire villages.)

This ayah addresses multiple senses. We have grain for sustenance, but we also have الرَّيْحَان - fields of flowers and fragrant plants. Functionally, flowers need only serve their biological purpose: enabling bees to extract nectar for honey production and facilitating plant reproduction. But Allah ﷻ made them beautiful to look at, with countless colors, patterns and designs.

Consider how flowers inspire human creativity. They influence interior and exterior design, perfume creation, fashion with floral prints on everything from rugs to curtains to clothing. Our artistic expression draws inspiration from the creativity Allah ﷻ already placed on this earth for us.

These ayahs present a powerful argument. Some people say, “I’ll only believe when I see proof.” Allah’s response is: What have you been looking at all this time? These signs have always been here for you to observe. Look at the variety of tasty fruits, the gift-wrapped dates, the fragrant farmlands, the beautiful flowers. Where do you think all this came from? Why would you assume it exists without purpose?

The Quran establishes two kinds of revelation, two types of messages from Allah ﷻ. The first message comes from creation itself - the natural world screaming with signs of a Creator. The second message is verbal revelation - the Quran and previous scriptures. When you truly appreciate the creation around you, you naturally begin seeking answers: Who is this God who placed all these gifts here? How do I thank Him? Those questions lead you to seek revelation.

But someone who remains oblivious to the gifts surrounding them never asks those questions. They never look for revelation because they don’t even acknowledge they’ve received anything worth being grateful for.

Ayah 13

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

This is the central refrain of Surah Ar-Rahman, repeated 31 times throughout. Let’s unpack its profound meaning.

The word ءَالَآء is plural of أَلْي , meaning favors, blessings, miracles, wonders, amazing things or accomplishments. The phrase بِأَىِّ means “which” or “how many.” So Allah ﷻ asks: How many more of these amazing gifts, these clear signs, these overwhelming blessings are you going to deny?

The word تُكَذِّبَانِ doesn’t just mean “reject” - it means to call something a lie, to be in denial of its reality. And notice the dual form: رَبِّكُمَا - “your Lord” addressing two, and تُكَذِّبَانِ - “you both deny.” Two entities are being addressed, though they haven’t been explicitly named yet. This mystery will be resolved later in the surah.

The question challenges fundamental ingratitude and willful blindness. How much more can you be oblivious to? How much more self-absorbed can you become? The problem isn’t just intellectual - it’s emotional and psychological.

One dimension of faith is precisely this: the emotional capacity to recognize gifts and feel gratitude. Imagine two people. One person looks around and sees blessings everywhere, feeling compelled to find and thank the Giver. The other person sees the same things and says, “What favor? I’m entitled to all this. Nobody needs to be thanked.”

This sentiment - wanting to thank someone who has done something for you - lies at the heart of Islam’s message. The Quran itself begins with praise and gratitude: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ . The starting point of Islam’s conversation is gratitude. If you’re not seeking to be grateful, the entire message becomes irrelevant.

This isn’t just a problem for non-Muslims. Many Muslims today, especially in wealthy societies, struggle with entitlement. We live with luxuries most people on Earth don’t enjoy: reliable cars, consistent electricity, clean water, healthcare access, maintained roads, law and order. We take so much for granted.

Each generation is raised with more luxury than the previous one. Parents who walked miles to school now drive their children everywhere. Families who considered meat a rare treat now throw away leftovers without thought. When you receive gifts repeatedly, you start taking them for granted. Worse, you start feeling you deserve them.

Give a child a toy every day, then skip one day. What happens? The child becomes upset, feeling wronged. But adults do this too. The air conditioning stops working in our car for one day and our entire world falls apart. One minor inconvenience and we spiral into complaint.

Interestingly, people with more luxury tend to complain more, while those with less remain more grateful. It takes very little to make a genuinely poor person happy. But wealthy people? The statistics from the 1990s showed the highest suicide rates occurred in the richest counties of the United States. The wealthiest nations, like Sweden, reported alarming suicide rates. Why? Because wealth breeds entitlement. You’re never satisfied. You’re always focused on what more you could have had.

We might teach our children to memorize surahs and learn about tawheed, but if we fail to instill genuine gratitude - the core of Islam - we’re missing something essential. When a child can’t go to a friend’s house and responds with “You never let me do anything! I’ve never had a day of happiness!” they reveal a dangerous lack of perspective. Tell someone in a developing country that your child has their own room and they’ll be shocked at such luxury.

When this core human value - the urge to be grateful - disappears, people stop looking for someone to thank. Instead, they constantly search for something to complain about. This erodes faith itself. Iman weakens. Young teenagers begin questioning, “I don’t know if there’s a God” - not because of sophisticated philosophical arguments, but because of their emotional state.

Often, philosophical doubts are directly tied to emotional states and material circumstances. People in the most difficult situations frequently have the strongest faith. Why? Some cynically say, “Religion is for poor people who have nothing else to hold on to. If they had money like us, they wouldn’t need God.” This attitude reflects exactly the problem this surah addresses.

The wealthy complainers in Makkah looked at the young, poor companions who joined the Prophet ﷺ and dismissed them: “What do these kids know? Religion is just for them.” This arrogance stems from feeling entitled, and entitlement comes from believing you deserve what you have - that you’ve earned your position through your own credentials, wealth, social status or intelligence.

Ayah 14

خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن صَلْصَـٰلٍ كَٱلْفَخَّارِ

He created man from clay like pottery.

After reminding us of countless blessings and questioning our ingratitude, Allah ﷻ delivers a humility check. You think you’re entitled? Let Me remind you of your origins.

The word صَلْصَال refers to dried clay that makes a ringing or crackling sound when touched - soil that has become completely barren and useless. Imagine land that has experienced heavy rain followed by intense heat. The ground cracks into plates. When you walk on it, these dried clay plates crackle under your feet. You can pick them up and crumble them in your hand. No plant grows there anymore. This soil has become utterly useless.

The phrase كَٱلْفَخَّار - “like pottery” - reinforces this imagery. When pottery is molded and then broken, can you reuse that clay for anything? No. It’s finished, done, unusable. That broken pottery represents something that has lost all value and purpose.

Allah ﷻ says: Don’t ever think you’re entitled to anything. You’re made from useless soil, like broken pottery clay. That’s your reality.

The wealthiest person and the poorest person are made from identical ingredients. We all return to the same earth. This is the great equalizer, the ultimate humility check.

Allah ﷻ establishes a profound correlation here. The surah presents two contrasting qualities: humility versus arrogance, gratitude versus ingratitude. But these aren’t separate pairs - they’re interconnected. Gratitude is directly tied to humility. When you’re humble, you recognize gifts as gifts and feel grateful. Arrogance, on the other hand, connects directly to ingratitude. When you’re arrogant, you see everything as something you deserve, something you’re entitled to, eliminating any sense of gratitude.

By reminding human beings of their humble origins - useless clay, broken pottery - Allah ﷻ aims to make us humble. And once we become humble, hopefully we’ll become what we’re meant to be: grateful.

This is the wisdom of placing this ayah here. After listing countless blessings and questioning our denial of them, Allah ﷻ addresses the root cause: arrogance and entitlement. The cure? Remember where you came from. Remember that everything you have is a gift, not a right. From this place of humility springs genuine gratitude - and from gratitude springs faith.

Ayah 15–29 · linked video: 07. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 15-29)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 15-29

Ayah 15

وَخَلَقَ ٱلْجَآنَّ مِن مَّارِجٍ مِّن نَّارٍ

Having discussed the creation of human beings from clay, Allah ﷻ now reveals the origin of the jinn: “And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire.” The word مَارِجٍ refers to the invisible, fused part of a flame. When you light a stove and see a blue flame, there’s an invisible portion underneath that you cannot see with your eyes. This unseen part is still fire, still burning, but it remains hidden from view. This is marij—that which is fused with the flame yet unseen. From this hidden element of fire, Allah ﷻ created the jinn.

Ayah 16

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Now we discover the meaning of “both of you” in this recurring question: “Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?” Allah ﷻ addresses both human beings and jinn. The remarkable aspect here is that Allah ﷻ mentions “both of you” before He even introduced us to the existence of the jinn in this passage. He mentions the duality before revealing who the second party is. Why?

Allah ﷻ caught the jinn off guard. They were listening at the time of revelation, and He informed the human beings: “By the way, I know the jinn have been listening.” This wasn’t arbitrary. When non-Muslims become hostile toward a prophet—not merely disbelieving but actively opposing—an army of jinn comes to aid them, fueling their animosity. The human enemies work in collaboration with unseen helpers, though the humans themselves remain unaware of this partnership. This is a pattern Allah ﷻ has established: prophets face enemies, and those enemies receive assistance from the jinn.

So Allah ﷻ addresses the most stubborn, the most hostile of the disbelievers, and simultaneously addresses their unseen helpers, declaring: your problem is the same. You are both ungrateful creatures. He confronts both their origins, then asks: which favors of your Master will you then deny?

Ayah 17

رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَغْرِبَيْنِ

“The Lord of the two Easts and the Lord of the two Wests.” What does this mean? There are several layers of understanding.

One meaning refers to the seasons. The sun doesn’t rise from the exact same position on Earth throughout the year. Over six months, it gradually shifts its rising point, then returns over the next six months. A farmer looking out his window for sunrise would, six months later, see it coming through the adjacent window. This gives us two Easts and, consequently, two Wests. Thus, one interpretation is that Allah ﷻ is the Master of all seasons.

Another interpretation considers human perspective. When you look at the eastern horizon, you see from northeast to southeast—the entire expanse of the East. Similarly with the West. The “two Easts” and “two Wests” encompass as far as your eye can see in either direction. Allah ﷻ is Master of everything within your visible horizon.

Ayah 18

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?”

Ayah 19-20

مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ يَلْتَقِيَانِ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخٌ لَّا يَبْغِيَانِ

But His dominion extends beyond what your eyes can see, into the depths: “He released the two seas, meeting side by side. Between them is a barrier which they do not transgress.”

When two bodies of water collide—one dark, one light; one oily or sandy, the other crystal clear—you can see a distinct line where they meet. They don’t mix despite running into one another. Allah ﷻ has placed between them a بَرْزَخ , a barrier that cannot be crossed. Neither body of water rebels against this divine boundary.

Water is an unstoppable force. When it floods a city, breaking through levees, it doesn’t care about buildings, structures or streets. It’s raw, overwhelming power. Yet when two massive bodies of this unstoppable force clash, Allah ﷻ defines the line and they don’t cross it. This demonstrates His power over these magnificent elements.

The comparison to human beings is clear: if Allah ﷻ can control one of the most powerful entities on Earth—water in unimaginable volumes—do you think He lacks power over you? Even these mighty forces don’t cross the line when Allah ﷻ sets one for them.

Ayah 21

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?”

Ayah 22

يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا ٱللُّؤْلُؤُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ

Even as this confrontation happens between two kinds of water clashing into one another, “From both of them emerge pearl and coral.”

When this ayah was revealed, some who were exposed to marine life—sailors and travelers—objected. They claimed that only salt water produces pearls, not sweet river water, and thus declared the ayah false. But they were limited by their experience. They had no knowledge of other parts of the world where sweet water does indeed produce pearls, like the river pearls found in the Mississippi and elsewhere.

The deeper point is this: Allah ﷻ is Master of as far as you can see, but the oceans and rivers extend beyond your limited experience. He reveals realities about the depths that you cannot access on your own.

Ayah 23

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?”

Ayah 24

وَلَهُ ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْمُنشَـَٔاتُ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ

The phrasing here is beautiful: “And to Him belong the ships elevated in the sea like mountains.”

الْجَوَارِ comes from the word to flow, to run, to sail, to cruise. Allah ﷻ says He alone owns the cruising ships, the sailing ships moving at rapid pace. But why does He say He alone owns them?

When a ship sits at the docks, it’s under control—safe, anchored, with people boarding and unloading. Everything is manageable. But once the ship leaves the dock and heads into open water, sailors know the reality changes. “It’s in God’s hands now,” they say. “Godspeed.” Even the disbeliever recognizes that in the middle of the ocean, you are at the mercy of forces beyond your control. Allah ﷻ declares: He alone owns the ships as they sail.

الْمُنشَـَٔاتُ means elevated to great heights. These ships appear like mountains or flags from a distance—so massive that people might mistake them for islands until they draw closer. The implication is that these are incredibly heavy vessels. In our time, think of aircraft carriers: entire airports floating in water, cruising at high speeds, carrying helicopters and airplanes.

Consider the contrast Allah ﷻ presents: tiny pearls at the bottom of the ocean, and gigantic vessels on top. If you stood on one of these sailing vessels holding a pearl and threw it into the ocean, what happens? The pearl sinks to the bottom. It makes you marvel: if Allah ﷻ wants something to float, it floats—no matter how massive. And what He wants to keep sunk stays at the depths—no matter how small. His control determines what rises and what descends.

Ayah 25

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?”

The connection is profound: you board that heavy ship, hoping it doesn’t sink, only to retrieve that tiny thing that sank to the ocean floor.

Ayah 26-27

كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ

After discussing worldly affairs, Allah ﷻ makes an universal statement that every believer should memorize: “Everyone upon it is perishing. But the Face of your Lord will remain, Owner of Majesty and Honor.”

The word فَانٍ means designed to die, meant to perish, meant to discontinue. Allah ﷻ designed us in a way that we were meant to go. This isn’t a flaw in our creation—it’s intentional design.

An atheist once challenged me: “You believe in God, but look at His failed products. We get old, our skin wrinkles, our eyesight fails, our hearing weakens. Diseases attack us, our backs give way, our muscles weaken, and eventually we fall apart and die. Some God—He couldn’t make a better product?”

My response: the concept of فَانٍ shows He designed us this way deliberately. When humans design products—iPhones, laptops, cars, houses—they want them to last forever. They offer warranties to convince you of longevity. Yet nothing lasts forever. But manufacturers still try to extend the lifespan.

Allah ﷻ designed the human being to age, weaken and die. Our mortality was installed into our operating system by design. This body we operate is essentially a rental property—and we’re not even paying rent. But it’s not permanent housing. We will leave this home.

Allah ﷻ wrapped our soul with this body, then wrapped the body with clothes, the clothes with our house, the house with our neighborhood, the neighborhood with the city, the city with the country, the country with the planet, and the planet with the solar system. Eventually all these layers will be undone, unfolded, and all that remains is the entity inside you.

This mortality is what the ungrateful person forgets, refuses to think about. It’s the saddest reality for those who deny the Hereafter, because they never found—never acknowledged—Someone who can give life after death. When they hit their forties, fifties or sixties, and mortality sets in, they become deeply depressed that life is about to end. That’s why they say “You only live once” and do whatever they want, caring nothing for consequences. “Who cares what happens afterward? I’ll be gone anyway.”

Allah ﷻ responds: كُلُّ مَنْ عَلَيْهَا فَانٍ—everyone will perish. But وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ—the Face of your Lord will remain.

Now examine His names: ذُو ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ—the Possessor of Majesty and Honor. الْإِكْرَامِ is different from karamah (nobility). Ikram means to give nobility, to bestow honor upon someone.

In the worldly sense, when is someone glorified? At the Nobel Prize ceremony, the Olympics, an NBA championship, an election, a graduation, a military medal ceremony. But for glory to exist, what’s necessary besides achievement? An audience. Someone must be there to witness and acknowledge the glory. If you win a championship alone in an empty stadium, you’d have to make the cheering sounds yourself.

The question arises: why does God need us to glorify Him constantly? Why does He need us to praise Him and recite His Beautiful Names? What does He get out of it? The disbeliever asks: “How can God be so self-obsessed? Why does He want us thanking Him all the time?”

This ayah answers that question. Allah ﷻ says: everyone will be dead, wiped out. And then we’ll talk about My Majesty and Honor. It will still be there. For us, glory and honor cannot exist unless someone else exists to give it to us. But for Allah ﷻ, He declares: I don’t need you to be glorified. I don’t need you to be honored. I already own it. Even after you’re gone, I’ll still own it.

The word “need” is removed from this picture entirely. Allah ﷻ advises us to praise Him, to be grateful—but the beneficiary of that praise is us, not Him. Elsewhere in the Quran, He says: “If you are grateful, I will increase you even more.” You’re enjoying the gifts you have on this Earth? You want more? Be grateful. The only beneficiary of gratitude is the human being.

Ayah 28

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?”

If you’re thinking Allah ﷻ needs praise, the conversation now flips: let’s talk about what you need first, before we discuss what I need.

Ayah 29

يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِى شَأْنٍ

“Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him constantly; every day He is in a matter.”

Whose needs are being discussed now? The previous ayah established Allah’s freedom from need—He doesn’t need anything. This ayah establishes how needy we are. Everyone in the skies and the earth depends on Him.

The word يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ isn’t just about asking—it carries the meaning of dependency. The dependent one is called a sa’il because he’s in a position where he cannot help himself. Every moment, in every situation, all of creation is in a state of asking, of needing, of depending on Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 29–39 · linked video: 08. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 29-39)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 29-39

Ayah 29

يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِى شَأْنٍ

Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is upon some task.

The word يَسْـَٔلُهُۥ carries a profound dual meaning. It doesn’t just mean “asks” in the sense of making requests—it encompasses complete dependence. This ayah addresses two distinct categories of creation.

The believers consciously ask Allah ﷻ for their needs. We turn to Him for guidance, for safety, for acceptance of our worship, for the health of our children, for the success of our students. This is the asking of recognition and awareness.

But the reality goes deeper. Even the disbeliever who refuses to acknowledge Allah ﷻ is still dependent on Him. When someone is thirsty, their throat asks Allah ﷻ for water. When they’re short of breath, their lungs ask for air. Every cell in the body asks for blood to be pumped to it. The heart asks for its next beat. This dependence exists whether we acknowledge it or not.

Notice that Allah ﷻ says مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—whoever is in the heavens and the earth. The pronoun is “man” (whoever), not “ma” (whatever), because this ayah addresses conscious beings: human beings and jinn. Both populations, whether they live in the earth or the heavens, depend entirely on Allah ﷻ.

The ayah continues: كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِى شَأْنٍ—every single day He is involved in some task. The word شَأْن is uniquely significant in Arabic. It doesn’t just mean “matter” or “issue.” A sha’n refers to something that must be done by a specific person, something that cannot be delegated or substituted.

Think of a mother feeding her infant. She has a particular way of feeding, a specific timing, a method of burping the child afterwards. This is her sha’n. If the father offers to help, she might say, “Just play with him afterwards—let me handle the feeding.” This task is so specific to her that it cannot truly be passed to another.

Business owners understand this concept intimately. When you first start a business, it becomes like your child. Every critical detail needs your personal attention because no one else will care for it the way you do. These responsibilities are your shuyun (plural of sha’n), and you cannot simply hire someone to replicate your level of ownership and care.

Allah ﷻ is telling us that every single day, He is immersed in countless matters that only He can handle. This raises a question in the skeptical mind: “So God is busy?” This leads directly into the next ayah.

Ayah 30

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

How can you be ungrateful when He is constantly busy fulfilling your needs? Every breath, every heartbeat, every moment of consciousness is a favor being granted even as you might speak against Him.

Ayah 31

سَنَفْرُغُ لَكُمْ أَيُّهَ ٱلثَّقَلَانِ

We will attend to you, O prominent beings (humans and jinn).

Here Allah ﷻ employs divine sarcasm to address a dangerous misconception. The word سَنَفْرُغُ comes from فَرَاغ , meaning to free oneself up, to make time for something specific.

Imagine a classroom where students are completing an assignment. Most are working diligently, but two in the back are chatting, playing around, completely off-task. The teacher warns them while grading papers: “Come on, get to work.” But they think, “He’s busy grading—he won’t really get to us.” They continue their conversation, comfortable in the assumption that the teacher is too occupied to deal with them individually.

Then class ends. “Everyone else can take a break. Can I see you two in my office?” Suddenly, the entire dynamic shifts. The nervous realization hits: he wasn’t too busy. He was watching the whole time. The sweating begins. “Is everything okay?” “We’ll see. Just close the door.”

This is the psychology Allah ﷻ is addressing. Some people commit sins thinking, “Allah is managing the entire universe—black holes, meteor showers, wars, mass atrocities. Surely He won’t concern Himself with my little habit, my small transgression. He’s got bigger problems to handle.”

Allah ﷻ responds: You think I’m too busy? You believe there are too many of you for Me to keep track of? We will free Ourselves up just for you. This isn’t a promise—it’s a warning wrapped in irony.

The critical difference between Allah ﷻ and any worldly authority becomes clear here. If seventy students each ask me for five minutes, that’s nearly six hours. I would be exhausted trying to give each person individual attention. The higher someone rises in authority in the human world, the less direct access people have to them. A CEO of a major corporation doesn’t know every employee, every engineer, every store worker. He must create layers of management—regional managers, team leaders, supervisors—establishing a hierarchy where most people answer to someone just above them, who answers to someone above them, and so on.

This hierarchical psychology is actually the foundation of shirk (associating partners with Allah). “I don’t need to deal with Allah directly. I’ll just maintain this one connection, this intermediary. They’ll handle my relationship with God.”

But this surah demolishes that notion entirely. You will be dealing with Him directly. The One whose Book wasn’t enough for you will ask you Himself about that on Judgment Day.

Ayah 32

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

Now imagine: Allah ﷻ has made time for all of humanity on Judgment Day. He repeats the question: How ungrateful can you still be? What happened to that dismissive attitude? The teenager’s “whatever” won’t exist anymore. That word—the verbal embodiment of apathy and disregard—represents everything opposite to contemplating Allah’s favors.

“Whatever” is the response people give when someone tries to offer advice or warning. It’s the ultimate dismissal, caring so little that you won’t even finish your sentence properly. The modern version is even more abbreviated, as if the speaker can’t be bothered to complete even that small word.

On Judgment Day, there will be no “whatever.” No casual dismissal. No oblivious chewing gum while emerging from graves. Everyone will be fully, painfully aware.

Ayah 33

يَـٰمَعْشَرَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ إِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَن تَنفُذُوا۟ مِنْ أَقْطَارِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنفُذُوا۟ ۚ لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ

O company of jinn and mankind, if you are able to pass beyond the regions of the heavens and the earth, then pass. You will not pass except by authority.

Notice that jinn are addressed first here. Throughout the Quran, sometimes human beings are mentioned first, sometimes jinn. Each placement has wisdom. In this context, jinn are mentioned first because they have greater capability for attempting escape. They can move through dimensions and spaces that humans cannot.

The scene is Judgment Day—the gathering has begun. Allah ﷻ issues what appears to be an open challenge: “If you’re capable of crossing beyond the boundaries of the heavens and the earth, go ahead. Run away.”

The image is like a police chase in a movie. The criminal runs, jumps fences, climbs buildings, leaps between rooftops, knocks over obstacles, flips over cars, scrambles across train tracks. The police struggle to keep up. They shout, “Freeze! Stop or I’ll shoot!”

But now imagine the police taunting instead: “Is that the fastest you can run? My grandmother could run faster! Come on, put some effort into it!” This sounds absurd, doesn’t it? Because if the police are mocking the criminal’s escape attempt, it means they’re not worried at all. They know he’s completely surrounded. They know exactly where he’s going. The chase is already over; they’re just letting him exhaust himself.

This is the announcement made on Judgment Day. Don’t run—or actually, go ahead and run if you can. Knock yourself out. You’re going nowhere without proper authorization. لَا تَنفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ—you cannot pass except with sultan (authority, documentation, permission).

Some have misunderstood this ayah to mean humans can never leave Earth, and when the moon landing occurred, certain scholars became confused, thinking this contradicted the Quran. But let’s take a breath and remember two critical points.

First, context: this ayah is describing Judgment Day, not life in this world.

Second, Allah ﷻ mentions السَّمَـٰوَٰتِ—the heavens, plural. Seven heavens exist, and the Quran explicitly tells us that the first, lowest heaven is decorated with stars. How far can we see stars with our most powerful telescopes? Billions of light years. We’re still looking at the first heaven. We haven’t come close to crossing its boundaries, let alone approaching the second heaven.

So relax. The Quran’s challenge remains unmet.

Ayah 34

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

Ayah 35

يُرْسَلُ عَلَيْكُمَا شُوَاظٌ مِّن نَّارٍ وَنُحَاسٌ فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ

There will be sent upon you flames of fire and smoke, and you will not defend yourselves.

The open challenge was given: run if you can. But some foolish jinn, some desperate humans will still try to escape anyway.

Then شُوَاظٌ —strikes of flame, meteor showers—rain down. نُحَاسٌ , copper or molten metal, is sent crashing upon them. Fire and copper rain from the sky.

Where does this meteor shower come from? The sky. And where were the jinn hoping to escape to? The sky. Now you cannot run toward where the bullets are coming from. That escape route is sealed.

The strikes land on earth, so running around the earth offers no safety either. You are trapped. فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ—and you cannot help yourselves. You cannot retaliate. You cannot avenge yourselves against these strikes.

Consider how many disaster movies Hollywood produces—narratives where the world approaches its end but humanity somehow survives anyway. They build a submarine. They stop the meteor from hitting Earth. They survive the great flood because human beings band together and believe in themselves. The constant theme: if they just believe in themselves enough, they can overcome even the forces of God.

This ayah shatters that delusion completely. No submarine. No last-minute scientific breakthrough. No power of positive thinking. فَلَا تَنتَصِرَانِ—you will not help yourselves.

Ayah 36

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

Ayah 37

فَإِذَا ٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً كَٱلدِّهَانِ

And when the heaven is split open and becomes rose-colored like oil.

This is the final image of Judgment Day’s major events before the focus shifts to individual accountability.

فَإِذَا ٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ—when the sky is torn apart, ripped open. The verb انشَقَّتِ conveys violent splitting, complete rupture.

فَكَانَتْ وَرْدَةً—and it becomes wardatan, rose-colored, blood red. كَٱلدِّهَانِ—like ad-dihan, like oil splatters, like the fat of a slaughtered animal.

The image Allah ﷻ employs is visceral: imagine slaughtering a lamb. You take this weak, helpless creature, you tear it open, you peel its skin. The entire sky is described like this fragile being whose skin is simply peeled away. The color? That dark maroon stain that oil makes when it jumps from a hot pan, or the color you see when an animal is slaughtered.

The sky itself—that seemingly permanent, unreachable expanse—is torn like paper, peeled like skin, colored like blood and oil. This is the backdrop against which individual judgment begins.

Ayah 38

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

Ayah 39

فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ

Then on that Day, no human or jinn will be asked about his sin.

Notice the sequence has reversed: now human beings are mentioned before jinn. The interrogation phase is beginning, and humans take precedence in this aspect of accountability.

But wait—this seems to contradict what we know about Judgment Day. Don’t we believe it’s a day when everyone will be questioned? Yet this ayah clearly states: لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ—they will not be asked.

The answer lies in understanding that Allah ﷻ describes Judgment Day in distinct phases. The first phase occurs before any arrests, before any books are opened, before any formal interrogation begins. At this initial stage, the results are already visible on people’s faces. You don’t even need to ask where someone stands—their guilt or innocence is written in their expressions.

Think about it: when someone commits a crime, their conscience knows. When the moment of reckoning arrives, before anyone says a word, before any questions are posed, the criminal already knows what they’ve done. That knowledge shows on their face. The fear, the dread, the recognition—it’s all there before a single question is asked.

This is phase one: your face tells the truth you tried to hide. Your sins don’t need to be asked about because they’re already on display. The interrogation, the opening of the book of deeds, the detailed accounting—all of that comes later. But at this first moment, when the sky has torn open and turned blood red, when the meteor showers have ceased and the escape attempts have failed, everyone stands exposed.

No questions necessary. The truth is already evident.

Ayah 39–45 · linked video: 09. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 39-45)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 39

Ayah 39

فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ

“So on that Day, no human or jinn will be questioned about his sin.”

The power of this ayah lies in what it reveals about the nature of Judgment Day. Allah ﷻ tells us that on that Day, no one will be asked about their sins—not humans, not jinn. This might seem puzzling at first. Why won’t there be questioning? The answer is profound: the questioning becomes unnecessary because the truth will already be evident on people’s faces.

Think about students taking an exam. Those who perform well carry a certain confidence, while those who fail already know it before the results are announced. The way they hand in their paper—face up or face down, rolled up, avoiding eye contact—tells the whole story. This is the reality of Judgment Day magnified a thousand times over. People will know exactly what they’ve done. The criminals will know their crimes. The righteous will know their deeds. The evidence won’t be hidden; it will be written across their very beings.

This is why questioning becomes redundant. When guilt or innocence is already manifest, when the reality of a person’s life is displayed for all to see, what need is there for interrogation? The Day of Judgment isn’t about discovery—it’s about facing what was always known but perhaps denied.

Ayah 40

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?”

Even in this context—speaking of a Day when sins need no questioning because they’re so evident—Allah ﷻ repeats His signature refrain from this surah. This repetition here carries special weight. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying: even the warning itself is a favor. Even knowing what’s coming is a blessing, because it gives us the chance to change now, before it’s too late.

Ayah 41

يُعْرَفُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ فَيُؤْخَذُ بِٱلنَّوَٰصِى وَٱلْأَقْدَامِ

“The criminals will be recognized by their marks, and they will be seized by the foreheads and the feet.”

Here Allah ﷻ explains how this recognition will occur. The word سيما refers to distinguishing marks or features—particularly on the forehead. The criminals will be recognized by their foreheads. This makes perfect sense when we understand that the face, especially the forehead, reveals what’s in the heart. Shame, guilt, arrogance, defiance—all of these inner realities manifest outwardly.

But then comes something even more striking: they will be seized by the foreheads and the feet. This particular description would have resonated powerfully with the original Arab audience. When they prepared an animal for roasting, they would grab it by the head and feet, tie it to a wooden spit, and place it over an open flame. This is how meat was prepared.

The imagery is devastating. These are people who insisted on living like animals—denying their spiritual nature, living only for immediate gratification, refusing to believe until they could see with their physical eyes. They rejected their humanity, the divine spark within them that calls toward something higher. The message is clear: you wanted to act like an animal? You denied the nobility Allah ﷻ placed in you as a human being? Then you’ll be treated accordingly.

Ayah 42

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?”

Ayah 43

هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى يُكَذِّبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ

“This is Hellfire which the criminals used to deny.”

Notice how Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “tilka Jahannam” (that is Hellfire), using a word for something distant. He says “hadhihi Jahannam”—this is Hellfire, using the word for something near and present. The linguistic choice is deliberate. Allah ﷻ wants us to feel the closeness of this reality, to understand that Hellfire isn’t some far-off abstract concept. It’s real, it’s there, and denying it doesn’t make it disappear.

Throughout this surah, the refrain has been about denying Allah’s favors—“Which of your Lord’s favors will you deny?” But here we see another dimension of that denial: the denial of Hellfire itself. This connects to a crucial point about the psychology of disbelief.

When someone becomes accustomed to comfort and luxury, when they live as a consumer who only accepts products they like, they approach religion the same way. “I don’t like the idea of Hellfire. Why would I accept a God who has this door of discomfort open? If you gave me a religion that only talks about things I like, maybe I’d find it more acceptable.”

But truth isn’t a product for sale. Truth is truth whether we like it or not. A person might hate the approaching flood, the earthquake, the tornado—but disliking these realities doesn’t stop them from coming. Similarly, you can hate the concept of Hellfire, but that hatred doesn’t negate its existence. If you truly believed it didn’t exist, you wouldn’t be bothered by its mention. The fact that it disturbs you reveals that on some level, you recognize its reality—you just don’t want to accept it.

And here’s the deeper issue: it’s not really about Hellfire at all. If you accept Hellfire as real, you have to change your behavior. And criminals—those who harm others, who live in corruption, who reject moral accountability—don’t want to change their behavior. They want a version of religion that requires no major modifications to their lifestyle while still promising them safety from punishment. They want convenience, not transformation.

Consider the context in which this surah was revealed. This is Makkan revelation, coming at a time when many of the detailed religious laws hadn’t yet been established. The five daily prayers had just been revealed. There was no obligation of hajj yet. No detailed food restrictions. No prohibition of alcohol. No commandments about modest dress. The ritual aspects of Islam that were later revealed in Madinah hadn’t come yet.

So what were these Makkan criminals being asked to change? Not religious rituals—those hadn’t been mandated yet. They were being asked to make moral changes. Stop the corruption. Stop the unjustified killing. Stop enslaving people and killing them without cause. Stop the theft and exploitation. Treat people with dignity. These are universal moral principles that any conscience recognizes.

When Allah ﷻ calls them criminals here, He’s not labeling them for violating ritual laws that didn’t exist yet. They’re criminals because they’re hurting humanity. They’re crushing the weak, exploiting the vulnerable, and refusing to be held accountable for their actions. That was Islam’s original challenge to them—not “don’t eat pork” but “stop oppressing people.” Understanding this context completely changes our perspective on what it means to be a criminal in Allah’s sight.

Ayah 44

يَطُوفُونَ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَ حَمِيمٍ ءَانٍ

“They will go around between it and scalding water, heated [to the utmost degree].”

The word يطوفون means to go in circles, to return to where you started. Picture this scene: the criminals are in the fire, and the heat becomes unbearable. In the distance, they see what appears to be water, and they rush toward it desperately, only to discover it’s حميم —boiling water.

But Allah ﷻ doesn’t just say boiling water. He describes it as آن , which refers to water that has reached the absolute peak of boiling. When you first heat water, small bubbles appear. Heat it more, and the bubbles grow larger. But if you keep heating it without turning off the stove, the bubbles become massive and violent—that’s the state of aan. It’s water boiling at its most extreme degree.

So they run from the dry heat of the fire to drink water, but this water is so scalding hot that it tears up their insides. The internal burning becomes worse than the external burning, and they flee back to the fire. But then the fire becomes unbearable again, and they run back to the boiling water. Back and forth, back and forth, eternally—never finding relief, never finding rest. Their existence becomes this terrible cycle of seeking escape from one torment only to fall into another.

Ayah 45

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?”

After this horrific description, Allah ﷻ asks again about His favors. And here we must pause to understand something profound: the very fact that we’re being told about Hellfire now, in this life, is itself a tremendous favor. Allah ﷻ is showing us this graphic, intense picture so we can be horrified now—while there’s still time to change—instead of experiencing that horror directly.

If the Quran hadn’t described Hellfire in such vivid, powerful terms, we might treat it as casually as it’s treated in other traditions, where hell becomes the subject of cartoons, movies, and jokes. When something is made light of, when it becomes entertainment rather than a serious warning, people lose their motivation to avoid it. They lose their sense of urgency about changing their lives.

But because Allah ﷻ has given us this full, three-dimensional view of Hell, we know what to avoid. And equally important, He’s given us a full, glorious view of Paradise—so we know what to strive for. We know what to look forward to.
