When we read about Paradise, we develop a yearning to see it, to experience it. When we read about Hell, we develop a sincere desire to be saved from it.

This detailed knowledge—of both destinations—is a favor that shapes how we live. It gives us clarity about what matters. It motivates us to make better choices. And one day, by Allah’s permission, when believers enter Paradise, they will see what Allah ﷻ meant by that tree, that waterfall, that castle. The seed of understanding planted by these descriptions will bloom into direct experience. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who see His promises fulfilled in the most beautiful way.

Ayah 46–54 · linked video: 10. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 46-54)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayahs 46-54

Ayah 46

وَلِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ جَنَّتَانِ

“And for the one who feared the standing before his Master, there are two gardens.”

Notice the beautiful transition Allah ﷻ makes here. The preceding ayahs described the terrifying reality of Judgment Day—criminals being dragged by their forelocks and faces, the overwhelming crowds, the accountability. These descriptions were meant to inspire fear in our hearts. Now Allah ﷻ immediately addresses that fear and says: if those ayahs scared you, if the thought of standing before Me moves you to be a better person, then congratulations—you get two gardens.

This shows us something profound about Allah’s mercy. Even the emotion of fear itself becomes a means of reward. Allah ﷻ didn’t describe punishment to destroy us, but to motivate us toward His gardens. It’s as though He created us specifically to be rewarded, to show us mercy rather than to punish us. Even when He mentions punishment, it’s to further qualify us for His rewards.

The word مَقَام is incredibly comprehensive. It means “to stand,” but encompasses three dimensions: the time of standing before Allah ﷻ, the place where that standing will occur, and the actual state of standing itself. The believer who truly feared this moment—who visualized standing before their Master, answerable for everything—that person is promised two gardens.

The word رَبّ reminds us of who we’re standing before: someone who has absolute authority over us. Right now, Allah ﷻ has authority over us, but we’re not immediately answerable to Him in this life. We get away with things without instant consequences. With our employer, if we show up late, we must explain ourselves that day. But with Allah ﷻ, there’s one day coming when every single transgression will be addressed at once. Nothing missed, nothing overlooked—a complete accounting. That prospect should be terrifying enough to drive us toward excellence.

The classical scholars discussed why Allah ﷻ mentions specifically two gardens. Some said one garden is for jinn and another for humans, since both groups are being addressed throughout this surah. Others explained that every believer receives two gardens, and these correspond to the two dimensions of faith: doing good deeds and avoiding bad deeds. This second interpretation reveals something important about our spiritual psychology.

Some people rationalize that as long as they don’t do major sins, they don’t need to actively pursue good deeds. “I don’t kill anyone, I don’t steal—so why should I have to pray?” Others think that because they perform acts of worship or give charity, they can justify a few bad deeds here and there. Both attitudes are problematic. The two gardens suggest we need a dual campaign in our personal lives: actively increasing our good deeds while simultaneously working to eliminate bad deeds. Allah ﷻ doesn’t demand perfection—He wants to see a genuine attempt from us in both directions.

Ayah 47

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

“Then which of the favors of your Master will you both deny?”

The refrain returns, but now its flavor has changed. It’s no longer just a challenge to disbelievers. Now it’s being recited to those entering the gardens of paradise. When the ayahs about Jannah begin, believers’ ears perk up—especially the early believers who heard these words during times of immense hardship.

Consider the historical context. The companions of the Prophet ﷺ were being beaten, tortured, expelled from their homes, socially boycotted. They lost their careers, their family ties, their social standing—everything familiar had been stripped away because of this one thing they refused to let go: Islam. Even their loved ones would say, “We care about you. Look at everything you’ve lost because of this Islam. Your life was comfortable before. Don’t you see this religion has ruined everything for you?”

The Sahaba (RA) could have seen themselves as miserable. But Allah ﷻ subtly shifts their perspective through these ayahs. He’s teaching them that getting to take those beatings, enduring that torture, standing alongside the Messenger of Allah ﷺ during those difficult moments—that wasn’t misery. That was an honor they should be eternally grateful for.

When they walk into Jannah and see its magnificence, they’ll realize: no matter how much we sacrificed, we weren’t even close to thanking Allah ﷻ enough for this. Even those who struggled at a level we can never compare to will feel they fell short in gratitude. That’s the attitude these ayahs cultivate.

How does this apply to us? Whenever we engage in Islamic work—whether studying the religion, teaching it, volunteering at a masjid or Islamic school, humanitarian efforts—there will always be challenges. People who don’t appreciate what we’re doing, personality clashes, demotivation. These things happen at every level, from Sunday school teachers to imams to board members. We might think, “This is voluntary anyway. I don’t need this stress. Let me just drop it.”

But we must see every opportunity to serve Allah’s religion as an honor, especially when it’s inconvenient. Those inconveniences are Allah’s way of testing our character. We’re put through difficulties because Allah ﷻ sees us as qualified for them. These aren’t reasons to walk away from good work—they’re reasons to persist, knowing Allah ﷻ rewards us even more as we persevere through the difficulties.

Ayah 48

ذَوَاتَآ أَفْنَانٍ

“Having many branches.”

The first thing you’ll notice when entering these gardens: massive, magnificent branches. The trees themselves haven’t even been described yet—you’re lost in contemplating these enormous branches. Because they’re so massive, you can picture humongous trees lining both sides of a walkway, their branches intertwining overhead, creating natural archways of greenery.

When trees are arranged like this with their branches covering the path, they create a sense of mystery. You can’t see what’s around the bend. The trees make the end of the walkway invisible, inviting you to explore further. You’re mesmerized by this greenery, these spectacular branches.

Naturally, if you see branches, you know there must be leaves and fruit on them. But because these branches are so high up—large branches mean tall trees—the fruit you glimpse is way up there. You’re not focused on fruit yet; you’re still exploring, taking in the magnificence of your surroundings.

Ayah 49

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Ayah 50

فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ تَجْرِيَانِ

“In both of them are two springs, flowing.”

As you continue exploring, you discover that each of these gardens contains two springs—four waterfalls altogether. You’re in your own private park, your own garden. When we say “garden” today, people picture a small backyard with some plants, maybe a decorative statue. But think of this as a vast national park, expansive and magnificent.

For those who live in places with natural beauty—California, Washington, and other states blessed with scenic landscapes—you know the waterfall is always the busiest spot. It’s always crowded; you can never take a picture without people in the background. The waterfall is the most scenic destination everyone wants to see.

But this is your own waterfall. Your personal waterfall. You don’t have to arrive at dawn to get a decent spot or settle for a view by the parking lot dumpster. You get your own space right by the flowing water.

The word تَجْرِيَانِ indicates water that flows softly, gently running. What do children do when they see running water? They run straight toward it. When we read these ayahs, we shouldn’t only imagine being in Jannah alone—that would be incomplete. We want to be there with our families. Any beautiful place you visit alone feels boring. We’d feel the absence of our loved ones, wishing they were there to share the experience.

I travel for work and visit many beautiful cities. When locals offer to show me downtown or take me to scenic spots, I usually decline and just go back after teaching. But if I travel with my kids, we go everywhere. Because when you visit a beautiful place alone, you just take a picture and send it back saying, “Wish you were here.” The background keeps changing, but you look the same in every photo—not particularly happy, because the people who matter aren’t there with you.

How much better is any trip when you’re with a friend or loved one? In Jannah, there are no concerns—no “watch out, you might drown,” no bugs requiring spray before exploring the woods, no seatbelts, no cranky children because they didn’t sleep on time. The kids can run freely. It’s all safe. It’s all good. Both parents can walk together without one rushing ahead while the other lags behind.

You can’t compare any outing in this world to Jannah, because all the worldly problems—the exhaustion as the day ends, the bugs, the inconveniences—none of that exists there. Only the joys remain, purified and perfected.

Ayah 51

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Ayah 52

فِيهِمَا مِن كُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ زَوْجَانِ

“In both of them are every kind of fruit, two varieties.”

After discovering the waterfalls and marveling at the trees, you start wondering about the fruits. In both these gardens, you’ll find every kind of fruit imaginable. The word فَاكِهَة refers to fruit that makes you smile. Every single fruit in Jannah will be delicious—not by someone else’s standards, but by yours specifically.

In this world, you like some fruits and dislike others. I personally can’t stand coconut—it feels like eating paper to me. I love grapes and strawberries, but find some fruits too sour even though others enjoy them. Not every fruit that enters your mouth is your absolute favorite. But in Jannah, every fruit will taste like the best fruit you’ve ever had. You’ll say, “This is it! Give me more of these.”

Then another one arrives, and you take a bite: “No, no—this one is even better! I thought the last one was perfect, but this is the one.” Yet it looks identical to the previous one. The Quran describes this phenomenon elsewhere: “It will appear the same to them, but it won’t be.” You’ll keep asking for more, and each time it gets better. How is that even possible? It keeps improving, endlessly.

The ayah mentions زَوْجَانِ —two variations of every fruit. You’ll have options, varieties, a complete selection of everything you could desire.

Ayah 53

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Ayah 54

مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍۭ بَطَآئِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ

“Reclining on furnishings whose linings are of silk brocade. And the fruit of the two gardens is hanging near.”

You’ve found the waterfalls, admired the magnificent trees, glimpsed the fruits. Now it’s time to relax. The word مُتَّكِئِينَ means to lean back comfortably. You’ll recline on فُرُش —luxurious furnishings, rugs, couches—laid out in this outdoor paradise.

Think about that: exquisite rugs outdoors, by a waterfall. In this world, you’d never put an expensive rug in your backyard. It would get covered in dust and ruined. But in Jannah, the outdoors has no dust, no dirt, no deterioration. The finest furnishings are spread out in the open air so you can relax in complete comfort. You lean back on cushions, surrounded by beauty.

The lining of these furnishings is made of إِسْتَبْرَق —the finest silk brocade. If the lining is pure silk, imagine what the exterior must be. The detail about the lining shows that even what’s hidden, what you recline against, is made of the most luxurious material imaginable.

And as you’re reclining there in complete comfort, وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ—the fruit of both gardens is دَانٍ , hanging low and near. Remember earlier when the fruit seemed high up on those massive trees? Now when you actually want the fruit, when you’re ready to enjoy it, it’s within easy reach. You don’t have to get up, don’t have to stretch or struggle. The fruit hangs close, accessible, ready for you to simply reach out and take it.

This is the life awaiting those who feared standing before their Master. Two complete gardens, flowing springs, magnificent trees, every variety of fruit continuously improving in taste, luxurious furnishings in a perfect outdoor paradise, and effortless access to every delight. This is what that fear qualifies you for—not because you were perfect, but because you genuinely tried to move toward good and away from evil, motivated by consciousness of standing before Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 54–66 · linked video: 11. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 54-66)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Commentary on Ayahs 54-66

Ayah 54

مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ فُرُشٍۭ بَطَآئِنُهَا مِنْ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ ۚ وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ

This ayah continues describing the first two gardens of Paradise, painting a vivid picture of comfort and luxury. The believers are reclining on furnishings whose inner linings are made of إِسْتَبْرَق - the finest, thickest silk.

The mention of the inner lining being made from this luxurious material carries a profound lesson about authenticity. In this worldly life, manufacturers and designers cut corners where they can. They invest in making the exterior beautiful while using cheaper materials for the interior - the parts no one sees. Mattresses, cushions and furniture are designed with beautiful outer coverings, but their inner stuffing is often low-quality filling. We see this in construction as well, where buildings are essentially steel warehouses dressed up with attractive facades of brick or stucco to create an illusion of quality.

But in Paradise, even the smallest details are genuine, inside and out. The inner lining - the part you wouldn’t normally see - is made from the most exquisite silk. This speaks to a fundamental difference between this world and the next: in Jannah, there is no artificiality, no deception, no cutting corners. Beauty in Paradise is not just surface-level; it permeates every aspect, even the hidden parts.

The ayah concludes with وَجَنَى ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ دَانٍ - the fruits of both gardens are within easy reach. Imagine relaxing on these comfortable furnishings and simply glancing at the fruit you desire. Without even needing to rise, the branches lower themselves to bring the fruit directly to your hand. This is the ultimate convenience - you need only look at what you want, and it comes to you.

Ayah 55

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny? This recurring refrain returns, reminding both humans and jinn of the blessings being described and challenging any denial of these promises.

Ayah 56

فِيهِنَّ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ

Now the surah addresses what often becomes a point of confusion and controversy: the description of spouses in Paradise. This ayah describes women who are قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ - those who lower their gaze modestly. The phrase captures a profound shyness, a quality that Islam considers part of a woman’s beauty and dignity.

The ayah continues: لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ - no human or jinn has ever touched them before. This purity is absolute and eternal. Notice that both humans and jinn are mentioned specifically. This isn’t simply stating the obvious - it’s actually extending the same promise to believing jinn: your spouse will have never been with another jinn, just as the human believers are promised spouses who have never been with another human.

Understanding the wisdom behind these descriptions requires examining the nature of temptation and reward. Islam places more restrictions on interactions between men and women than perhaps any other way of life. From dress codes to conversation guidelines, from the prohibition of khalwah (being alone with a non-mahram) to the detailed etiquette found in Surah An-Nur about entering homes and maintaining proper boundaries - even specifying times when children must knock before entering their parents’ room - the boundaries are comprehensive and serious.

For a young man living in a society saturated with immodesty, where he’s constantly exposed to temptation, these restrictions are challenging. He goes to college and encounters situations where barely-dressed women might approach him casually. His youth, his natural desires, his curiosity - everything within him might urge him to look, to engage, to pursue what seems readily available. Yet he lowers his gaze. He walks away. He says to himself, “Allah ﷻ is better for me. Allah ﷻ is better for me.” He holds himself back from what his peers consider normal, denying himself something that seems so accessible.

This is precisely why these descriptions appear in the Quran. Allah ﷻ doesn’t deny that we have temptations. He doesn’t ignore the reality that thoughts and desires come to us constantly. He acknowledges this reality. The Quran itself states, “Beautified for people is the love of that which they desire - of women and sons…” (3:14). This is human nature, and Allah ﷻ created us with it.

But in His perfect justice, Allah ﷻ says to those who restrain themselves in this world: Look how you held yourself back for My sake. Now, in Jannah, enjoy. The restrictions I placed on you in the dunya are lifted here. The desires you disciplined for My pleasure - here, I fulfill them in ways beyond your imagination. If you want to experience the party of Paradise, you must guard yourself carefully in this life.

Ayah 57

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 58

كَأَنَّهُنَّ ٱلْيَاقُوتُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ

These companions are described as كَأَنَّهُنَّ ٱلْيَاقُوتُ وَٱلْمَرْجَانُ - as if they were rubies and coral. This comparison to precious jewels carries multiple layers of meaning.

First, these gems must be discovered through effort - pearls require diving deep into the ocean, precious stones require digging deep into the earth. They represent hidden treasures that one seeks and then guards carefully. Second, and more importantly, jewels like rubies and pearls are timeless. They don’t deteriorate. They don’t lose their luster or value over time. Unlike things in this world that age and decay, these gems maintain their beauty perpetually.

This is actually describing the nature of the relationship itself. In this world, marriages go through changes. Those who have been married for years understand that the relationship evolves over time. If you’ve been married ten, fifteen, twenty years, it becomes difficult to remember what it was like in the beginning. Those first moments - the wedding night, that first shy glance, that first time sitting together when she lowered her gaze in beautiful modesty, and that shyness pierced straight through to your heart - those are some of the most beautiful moments of marriage.

But does that shyness last ten years into marriage? The dynamics change. Sometimes the tables turn completely. But in Jannah, Allah ﷻ promises that those initial feelings, that beautiful shy relationship, that sense of discovery and wonder - it becomes permanent. The first moments of marriage become eternal. This is what قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ signifies.

The comparison to precious jewels also addresses a reality of worldly relationships: sometimes the value seems to decrease over time. Couples may feel, “I don’t feel about you the way I used to. Something has changed.” People write songs about this loss. But in Paradise, like timeless jewels, the appreciation never diminishes. Your valuing of your spouse remains constant and even grows.

These descriptions should actually inspire our marriages in this life. We should strive to keep our relationships timeless, to continue valuing our spouses, to maintain shyness and romance in the marriage. When these elements start dying in a marriage, that’s when shaytan finds entry points. That’s when fitna creeps in and begins breaking the relationship apart. This is why maintaining love, friendship and that sense of special connection between husband and wife is so critical in the Sunnah.

Regarding an important question that arises: the Quran describes rewards for men, but what about women? What do women receive in Paradise? There’s a profound principle to understand here. Allah ﷻ created men and women differently, with different natures and different desires. Men are largely - not exclusively, but overwhelmingly - uniform in one particular aspect of desire. This isn’t something to be ashamed of; it’s how Allah ﷻ created them, and the Quran acknowledges it directly.

Women, however, are more complex and diverse in their desires and aspirations. An experiment conducted with teenagers illustrated this: when boys were asked what they would want if they could have anything without restrictions, the answers were remarkably uniform. But when girls were asked the same question, the responses varied tremendously - some wanted to be with their mothers, others wanted specific experiences, others wrote multiple answers then crossed them out and rewrote them. One common answer was simply, “It depends.” This diversity isn’t a weakness; it reflects a richer emotional and spiritual complexity.

The silence of the Quran on explicit descriptions of rewards for women isn’t an omission - it’s eloquence. There’s an Arabic expression: “Perhaps silence speaks more loudly than words.” Some rewards are so magnificent, so beyond our current comprehension, that they cannot be adequately described. In several places, the Quran describes certain deeds whose rewards are so great that Allah ﷻ simply says, “Its reward is upon Allah” - I’ll take care of it Myself. The reward transcends description.

Interestingly, this indescribable reward is promised for those who forgive others even when they have the power to take revenge. Such people receive compensation that cannot be put into words - Allah ﷻ simply says, “I’ll take care of them.”

Both men and women should rest assured: the Quran promises clearly, “And you will have therein whatever your souls desire, and you will have therein whatever you ask for” (41:31). Whatever you want will be there. And then some. And if later you think of something else you want, you can have that too. This door is open to both men and women equally.

Ayah 59

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 60

هَلْ جَزَآءُ ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ إِلَّا ٱلْإِحْسَـٰنُ

Should the compensation of excellence be anything less than excellence? Should you be given anything short of what you deserve?

This rhetorical question is powerful in its simplicity. If you lived with ihsan - excellence, beauty, spiritual refinement - in this world, if you embodied ihsan in your worship, your character and your restraint, then you deserve nothing less than ihsan in return. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: You earned this. This isn’t charity; this is what you deserve.

Ayah 61

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 62

وَمِن دُونِهِمَا جَنَّتَانِ

And besides these two gardens, there are two other gardens. The surah now hits a reset button, introducing a second pair of gardens. This isn’t simply repetition - this is an upgrade, a “version 2.0” of Paradise. The structure mirrors the first description but with intensified details, showing that the rewards of Jannah exist in levels, each more magnificent than the one before.

Ayah 63

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 64

مُدْهَآمَّتَانِ

These two gardens are described as مُدْهَآمَّتَانِ - dark green, nearly black from the intensity of their lushness.

Where the first description of Paradise began with large trees and expansive branches, this description goes a step further. These gardens are so thick with vegetation, so overwhelmingly verdant, that light barely penetrates through the canopy. Imagine walking through a forest so dense that only occasional sprinkles of sunlight pierce through here and there. Most of the garden is shrouded in this rich, dark greenness - not from darkness itself, but from the sheer abundance of foliage.

This image is striking. It suggests mystery, depth and overwhelming abundance. The greenery is so intense, so lush, that it appears almost black. This is already signaling that these gardens are more intense than the first pair described.

Ayah 65

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

Which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 66

فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ نَضَّاخَتَانِ

In both of these gardens are two springs, نَضَّاخَتَانِ - gushing abundantly, flowing with tremendous force.

Were there springs in the first gardens? Yes, they were described as تَجْرِيَانِ - flowing. But now the word نَضَّاخَتَانِ is used, which indicates springs that gush forth powerfully, perhaps as waterfalls or fountains that burst upward with force. This is a more intense, more spectacular description than what came before.

The pattern is clear: the second pair of gardens takes every element from the first and amplifies it. Where the first had flowing springs, these have gushing fountains. Where the first had abundant greenery, these are so lush they appear dark. Each layer of Paradise is more magnificent than the last, and Allah ﷻ continues asking after each description: Which of your Lord’s favors will you both deny?

Ayah 66–76 · linked video: 12. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 66-76)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 66

Ayah 66

فِيهِمَا عَيْنَانِ نَضَّاخَتَانِ

In this ayah, Allah ﷻ describes springs that are نَضَّاخَتَانِ - gushing with such force that sprinkles fill the air. This is a more powerful description than what appeared earlier in the surah. The word naddakh refers to water that gushes so intensely that you can feel the mist and sprinkles when standing near these waterfalls. This vivid imagery paints a picture of abundant, forceful water sources that demonstrate the elevated nature of this second level of Paradise.

Ayah 67

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?

Ayah 68

فِيهِمَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَنَخْلٌ وَرُمَّانٌ

This ayah presents something particularly interesting. While fruit (fakihah) was mentioned in the description of the first level of Paradise, here Allah ﷻ becomes more specific: فَـٰكِهَةٌ (all kinds of fruit), وَنَخْلٌ (date palms), and رُمَّانٌ (pomegranates). The specification of date palms and pomegranates is significant because these were two of the most beloved fruits among the Arabs.

We can understand from this that Allah ﷻ made specific customizations to this higher level of Paradise to address the particular preferences of the Arab companions of the Prophet ﷺ. The majority of the Companions were Arabs - they were the first generation, the سَابِقُونَ (the foremost ones). Allah ﷻ prepared something uniquely special for that generation in Paradise because they accomplished what no other generation had done: they accompanied the Prophet ﷺ during the most difficult mission ever undertaken in human history.

You’ll notice throughout this section that there are distinctly Arab elements - what some scholars consider a tribute to the Companions and their cultural context. This connects beautifully with the next surah, which describes the سَابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ (the first and foremost). When we think of the first and foremost in Islam, who immediately comes to mind? The Sahaba (RA).

Ayah 69

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?

Ayah 70

فِيهِنَّ خَيْرَٰتٌ حِسَانٌ

The word خَيْرَٰتٌ comes from خَيْر , meaning good. But it carries a deeper meaning - it refers to goodness that is chosen, selected. It also means “good choice” or “best choice.” The idea here is that these spouses are not assigned by someone else’s preference, but are exactly according to your own choice and liking. Everything about them - their physical beauty, their personality, their demeanor - is precisely what you want.

In this world, you can love your spouse deeply, but the reality is that there will always be some things you simply have to learn to live with. You love much about them, but certain habits or traits require patience and acceptance. That’s the nature of worldly relationships. But in Paradise, everything about your spouse is perfect - exactly as you desire in every way.

Then Allah ﷻ adds حِسَانٌ , from حَسَن , meaning beautiful. So Allah ﷻ combines خَيْرَات (goodness of character) with حِسَان (exceptional beauty) - a rare combination in this world. Often in the dunya, beauty and excellent character don’t come together easily, but in Paradise, this is the norm.

An important clarification: your spouse from the dunya will also be your spouse in Paradise. Some might worry about the baggage and difficulties from their earthly marriage, but understand that Allah ﷻ removes all rancor from the hearts in Paradise. Whatever scars you carry from your marriage in this world, whatever conflicts or painful memories - all of them completely disappear. You won’t remember the fights, you won’t carry the emotional baggage. Everything about him becomes perfect for you, and everything about her becomes perfect for you. It’s a complete fresh start.

The Quran does indicate that men will have more than one spouse in Paradise - this is undeniable from the text. However, we must understand that the emotional framework of Paradise is entirely different from this world. In the dunya, jealousy is natural. Even among the Mothers of the Believers, there was natural jealousy when they had to share the Prophet’s ﷺ time. The story of the honey incident stems from those very human feelings. But that sentiment of possessiveness and jealousy is completely removed in Paradise. The way we process emotions and understand relationships there operates on a completely different paradigm than what we experience here.

Ayah 71

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?

Ayah 72

حُورٌ مَّقْصُورَٰتٌ فِى ٱلْخِيَامِ

The word حُورٌ appears here, and interestingly, the next surah also uses this word once - this surah uses it for the higher level of Paradise while the next uses it for the سَابِقُون (the foremost), which makes perfect sense since the سَابِقُون are those who deserve the highest levels of Paradise.

The term حُور refers to a beautiful woman whose eyes are captivating. The word specifically describes eyes where the white part creates a startling, mesmerizing contrast with the black pupils. When you look into her eyes, you become transfixed - that’s what حُور means.

This is significant because in the description of the first level of Paradise, the spouses were described as قَاصِرَاتُ الطَّرْفِ (those who restrain their gaze) - avoiding eye contact with others. But now Allah ﷻ shifts the picture and says: here’s why you would truly want to make eye contact.

The word مَّقْصُورَٰتٌ means they hold themselves back, waiting in anticipation. And then comes something distinctly Arab-specific: فِى ٱلْخِيَامِ (in tents).

Now, what’s particularly Arab about tents? You can take the Bedouin out of the desert, but you can’t take the desert out of the Bedouin. Even today, when people from the Gulf states or royal families take vacations, what do they do? They set up air-conditioned tents in the middle of the desert. After thousands of years of civilization and development, this concept of tents representing luxury and comfort remains deeply ingrained in Arab culture. Allah ﷻ honored that cultural preference by offering it to them in Paradise.

Let’s explore the concept of مَقْصُورَات (waiting at home) for a moment, because it offers insight for married life in this world as well. We live in a modern era where both husband and wife are often working, studying, or managing multiple responsibilities. Yet there’s something powerful about a wife occasionally making special time - staying home, preparing something special for her husband, creating an environment where he eagerly anticipates coming home because he knows she’s waiting for him.

This relates to a broader discussion about Muslim women and the balance our religion requires. Islam wants Muslim women to embody seemingly contradictory qualities: confidence and education alongside extreme modesty and حَيَاء (shame/modesty). Fearlessness in engaging with the world, yet maintaining protective boundaries. These are challenging qualities to balance.

Consider the story of Musa (AS) in Surah Al-Qasas. When he arrives in Madyan, what does he see? Two young women waiting to water their flock, standing apart from a crowd of men. They’re waiting for the men to finish because they don’t want to engage with men of poor character. Did they say they shouldn’t be in the workforce? No - it was a necessity. Their elderly father couldn’t manage it, so they had to work. But they took precautions to guard their modesty while fulfilling their responsibility.

That balance - engaging the world while maintaining modesty - is crucial. These two sisters represent a combination of حَيَاء (modesty), confidence, and the ability to speak for themselves. When one of them returns to call Musa (AS), notice her direct, firm speech: لِيَجْزِيَكَ أَجْرَ مَا سَقَيْتَ لَنَا - “so that he may compensate you for watering our flock for us.” She’s tough, clear, establishing boundaries immediately. She speaks with confidence but maintains complete propriety.

These sisters represent the balance Muslim women need to find - not pushed to either extreme of being overly masculine in their confidence or so shy they cannot function in necessary worldly interactions. They demonstrate how to draw that line.

For fathers raising daughters, especially in Western society, the challenge is significant. Generally, American culture produces more outward confidence in children - they’re louder, more assertive. The harder task is cultivating حَيَاء (a sense of shame and modesty) in them.

The home should be made the most wonderful place to be, especially for girls until they complete high school. It should be their comfort zone, their happy place. When they go outside, they should look forward to returning home. Home should have everything that makes them happy - activities, projects, good food, space to be themselves. If that foundation of loving home and understanding modesty is built correctly during childhood, they’ll be prepared to engage the wider world appropriately when they enter university or the workforce.

Ayah 73

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?

Ayah 74

لَمْ يَطْمِثْهُنَّ إِنسٌ قَبْلَهُمْ وَلَا جَآنٌّ

The word يَطْمِثْ means to touch. Allah ﷻ emphasizes that no human being nor jinn has ever touched these spouses before. This highlights their purity and that they are exclusively for those who enter this level of Paradise.

Ayah 75

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?

Ayah 76

مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ وَعَبْقَرِىٍّ حِسَانٍ

As we reach the conclusion of the surah, Allah ﷻ returns to the image of reclining - مُتَّكِـِٔينَ (leaning back in comfort), which was mentioned for the first level of Paradise as well. But here He adds specific details: عَلَىٰ رَفْرَفٍ خُضْرٍ - upon green cushions or pillows arranged in rows.

The color green holds special significance. The Arabs didn’t see much of it in the desert landscape. Green was associated with beauty and life itself. When you see green in the desert, you think of water, you think of life and vitality. So green becomes the signature color of the elite Paradise - a color that represents the ultimate in beauty and luxury.

Then comes one of the most linguistically fascinating phrases in the entire Quran: وَعَبْقَرِىٍّ حِسَانٍ. The word عَبْقَرِىّ is constructed like “Pakistani” or “American” - it means “from Abqar.” But what is Abqar? In ancient Arab folklore, Abqar was considered the land of the jinn - like we might say “Mars” for aliens, representing a completely otherworldly place.

When Arab travelers would journey beyond Arabia to Syria, Greece, the Roman Empire, or Persia, and return with decorative items - a vase, artwork, fine crafts - these exotic items were called عَبْقَرِىّ. It was as if they had visited another world, beyond the realm of humans, and brought back something supernatural and beautiful. The modern equivalent would be imported art or goods - a handmade Persian rug, an imported chandelier, unique art pieces from distant lands.

People naturally want to decorate their homes with beautiful, exotic things, especially if that home is meant to last forever. The word عَبْقَرِىّ indicates that the decorative items in this Paradise will be of beauty never before seen - completely unique to each person. No one will look at your furnishings and say, “I have the same thing” or “I know where you got that from.” Every piece will be unprecedented, one-of-a-kind beauty.

Allah ﷻ pairs عَبْقَرِىّ with حِسَان (beautiful), which is important because not everything exotic or imported is necessarily beautiful - but these will be both extraordinarily unique and exceptionally beautiful. This is how Allah ﷻ concludes the description of the higher Paradise - with the image of eternal comfort, surrounded by unparalleled beauty that has never been witnessed before.

Ayah 77–78 · linked video: 13. Ar-Rahman (Ayah 77-78)

Surah Ar-Rahman: Ayah 77-78 Commentary

Ayah 77

فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ

So which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?

This refrain appears once more, reminding us that even in recognizing Allah’s ﷻ blessed names, we are experiencing His favor upon us.

Ayah 78

تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِى ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ

Blessed is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor.

The surah ends where it begins. At the very opening, Allah ﷻ presented a single word as an independent ayah—الرَّحْمَـٰنُ . The subject and predicate were separated, creating a moment of reflection focused entirely on that divine name. Now, at the conclusion, the surah returns to glorifying Allah’s ﷻ name, specifically highlighting Rahman—the Overwhelmingly Merciful.

Understanding Tabaraka

The verb تَبَارَكَ deserves careful examination. It comes from the root بَرَكَة , commonly translated as “blessing.” However, the technical meaning of barakah is الخير والزيادة في الخير—goodness and increase in goodness beyond what is expected. It represents outcomes that far exceed the anticipated results of an action.

In physics, we learn that every action produces an equal but opposite reaction. However, an action infused with barakah does not produce merely an equal reaction—it generates consequences that far surpass expectations. Barakah empowers something with the ability to create results beyond imagination.

Examples of Barakah in Action

Consider a simple act: teaching a child the Arabic alphabet. The immediate result is that the child learns their letters. But when that action contains barakah, that child may grow to become a great teacher of the Quran, a scholar who guides thousands to Islam. All of this stems from what appeared to be a small beginning—teaching the basic letters—because that action carried barakah within it.

In the story of Musa (AS), his mother fed him as an infant. The expected result of this action is simply that the child’s hunger is satisfied. Yet this action, blessed with barakah, led to the child and mother being reunited, the safety of their extended family and ultimately the liberation of an entire enslaved people. These monumental consequences flowed from the simple act of a mother feeding her child, because that action carried barakah.

The Barakah of Allah’s ﷻ Name

Allah ﷻ tells us that there is a name of His that has been specially empowered with barakah. When you invoke this name and undertake an action, that action will carry barakah within it. According to this surah, that name is Rahman.

This explains why we begin every action with the phrase “Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim.” At the heart of this phrase lies the name Rahman. We start every good deed with this invocation, hoping optimistically that Allah ﷻ will place barakah in our efforts—that the good emerging from our actions, both for ourselves and others, will extend far beyond our imagination and expectations.

A Call to Optimism

Because we believe in Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim, we should never underestimate the value of good deeds performed for Islam. The world around us may appear filled with overwhelming challenges—ignorance, division, corruption, powerlessness. The educational state of the Ummah, political turmoil, economic struggles, social problems—all of these combined can make circumstances seem hopeless.

Yet as a matter of faith, pessimism is not an option for the believer. Allah ﷻ has given us this name—Ar-Rahman—and with it, the promise of barakah. When we invoke this name and begin our efforts, only Allah ﷻ knows how far the good will extend from our deeds. This must shape the attitude of every Muslim.

The Companions (RA) understood this principle deeply. They never underestimated the value of their efforts, even when conversions to Islam were not immediately visible. They persisted because they recognized that their efforts carried barakah, and they did not need to witness the results with their own eyes to know this truth.

The Patience of Barakah

Some plants emerge from the soil immediately after planting. Others take time. Some require patience and seasons to pass before they break through the ground. The barakah in our actions works similarly. Some results manifest quickly, while others remain dormant, waiting for their appointed time. Seeds planted today may not sprout until we are long gone, but that does not diminish their value. This is the nature of barakah—sometimes it waits, gathering strength, until the moment Allah ﷻ has decreed for it to flourish.

This is the profound message of this closing ayah: that the blessed name of your Lord—the Owner of all Majesty (الْجَلَالِ) and all Honor (الْإِكْرَامِ)—carries within it the power to transform our smallest efforts into legacies of immeasurable good. We begin with His name, we trust in His promise and we remain optimistic about outcomes only He can see.
